title: Anatomy of a Test
blurb: Every good unit test has the same shape: Arrange, Act, Assert. Put the steps in the order they run — hover the ⓘ if a term is fuzzy.

docs.label: Microsoft Learn — Unit testing best practices (Arrange-Act-Assert)

glossary.Arrange: Set up the world: create the object under test (the SUT), build the inputs, and configure any mocks. Nothing is verified yet.
glossary.Act: Call the one method you're testing — the single action this test is about.
glossary.Assert: Check the outcome against what you expected. This is the line that makes the test pass or fail.
glossary.Set up (create SUT + data): The Arrange phase: build the system under test and its input data before anything runs.
glossary.Dispose / clean up: Release anything the test allocated (files, connections, the SUT). Runs last, after the checks are done.

levels.title: L1 · Arrange, Act, Assert
levels.prompt: Put the three phases of a unit test in the order they run.

failStates.title: Nothing to assert yet
failStates.explain: You checked the result before calling the method — there's no result to check. Act (call the method) has to run before Assert.

failStates.title: Acted before arranging
failStates.explain: You called the method before building its inputs and the object under test. Arrange sets the stage first.

levels.title: L2 · Add cleanup
levels.prompt: A test that allocates resources releases them last. Order all four steps.

failStates.title: Cleaned up too early
failStates.explain: Disposing before you assert can tear down the very state you need to check — the assertion may read a closed connection or a disposed object.

failStates.title: Acted before setup
failStates.explain: You called the method before creating the system under test and its data. Setup (Arrange) comes first.
